title: Ham and cheese omelette
subtitle: A classic French omelette filled with ham and melted cheddar.
excerpt: Eggs beaten and cooked in butter over high heat, folded around a filling of diced ham and grated cheddar. Done in under three minutes. The kind of thing that separates cooks who understand heat from those who don't.
sourceNotes: The French rolled omelette is one of the traditional tests of a cook in classical cuisine: Escoffier and others considered it a demonstration of skill because the technique is demanding and unforgiving of hesitation. The version here uses the French method rather than the British flat omelette: the pan must be very hot, the eggs moved constantly, and the fold executed decisively. Ham and cheddar is a British filling variant; the French would more likely use gruyère.

content.text: The pan must be properly hot before the butter goes in, and the butter must be foaming before the eggs go in. A too-cool pan produces a thick, rubbery omelette. The whole process should take about 90 seconds from egg to plate.
Use a small non-stick frying pan, 20 cm is ideal for a two-egg omelette. The eggs should cover the base without being too thin.
Break {{eggs}} into a bowl. Season with a pinch of salt and pepper. Beat well with a fork until the yolks and whites are fully combined and slightly frothy. No milk, no cream.

Heat the pan over high heat until hot. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and swirl to coat. When the butter foams, pour in the eggs. Immediately start stirring with a rubber spatula in quick circular motions, shaking the pan at the same time. Stop stirring when the eggs are just set but still slightly wet on top. Scatter the {{prosciutto}} of ham and {{cheddar}} of cheddar over one half. Tilt the pan and fold the plain half over the filled half. Slide onto a plate. Eat immediately.

items.fix: The pan must be properly hot before the butter goes in. The whole cook should be fast and high.
items.cause: Pan was not hot enough, or cooked too slowly.

items.fix: Use a pan with a good non-stick surface. Replace pans with scratched coatings.
items.cause: Non-stick coating is damaged, or not enough butter.

items.fix: Have the filling at room temperature, or warm the ham briefly before folding in.
items.symptom: Filling is cold in the middle

items.fix: A French omelette should be golden yellow with no browning. If browning appears, reduce the heat slightly next time.

content.text: The omelette occupies a specific place in culinary tradition as a technique-revealer: it cannot be rescued if done wrong and cannot be made to look good if hurried. Auguste Escoffier listed omelettes among the preparations a cook must master before anything else, because the skills involved (reading heat, moving eggs, judging doneness) transfer to a hundred other dishes. The ham and cheese filling is secondary. Getting the fold right is the point.
